title: Action potential: K+ permeability peaks at repolarization
topic: Resting membrane potential and action potential
vignette: Neurophysiologists are studying recordings of the membrane potential from a giant squid axon. A portion of their recordings is shown as a tracing of membrane potential versus time over a single action potential, with five labeled points: point A on the flat resting baseline just before the upstroke, point B on the steep rising (depolarization) phase, point C at the peak/overshoot, point D on the falling (repolarization) phase, and point E on the late tail that dips slightly below baseline before returning. The membrane is most permeable to potassium ions at which of the following points?

clues.note: The squid giant axon is the classic Hodgkin-Huxley preparation for recording action potentials. Context only - it tells you this is a textbook action-potential tracing.

clues.text: point A on the flat resting baseline just before the upstroke
clues.note: At REST the membrane is most permeable to K+ RELATIVE to Na+ (leak channels hold the -70 mV resting potential). This tempts you to pick A - but the ABSOLUTE peak of K+ permeance comes later, during repolarization.

clues.text: point B on the steep rising (depolarization) phase
clues.note: Depolarization is driven by voltage-gated Na+ channels opening; Na+ permeability (not K+) is what surges here as Na+ rushes in.
The overshoot (~+35 mV) is the moment Na+ permeability is maximal and voltage-gated K+ channels are just beginning to open - not the K+ peak.

clues.text: point D on the falling (repolarization) phase
clues.note: Repolarization: Na+ channels inactivate and voltage-gated K+ channels open widely, so K+ efflux - and K+ permeance - reach their MAXIMUM, exceeding even the resting value. This is the answer.

whatMatters: Trick of absolute vs relative permeability. At REST (A) K+ permeability dominates over Na+ - but the ABSOLUTE maximum of K+ permeance occurs during REPOLARIZATION (D), when voltage-gated K+ channels open en masse and K+ efflux exceeds the resting level. Answer D. B/C are the Na+-permeability phase; E (hyperpolarization) is those K+ channels closing.
strategy: Map each labeled point to a phase: A = resting, B = depolarization, C = overshoot/peak, D = repolarization, E = hyperpolarization. 'Most permeable to K+' = where voltage-gated K+ channels are maximally open = repolarization (D). Don't reflexively pick resting (A): there K+ only dominates RELATIVE to Na+; absolute K+ permeance is higher during repolarization.

eliminations.label: Point A (resting) - relative, not absolute, K+ peak
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: at rest (-70 mV) the membrane is held by K+ leak channels, so it is far more permeable to K+ than to Na+. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is RELATIVE dominance; the ABSOLUTE maximum of K+ permeance is higher during repolarization, when voltage-gated K+ channels add to the leak. ✅ Correct if: asked when K+ permeability most exceeds Na+ permeability.

eliminations.label: Point B (depolarization) - the Na+ phase
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the upstroke is voltage-gated Na+ channels opening with rapid Na+ influx. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Na+ permeability, not K+, surges here. ✅ Correct if: asked when the membrane is most permeable to sodium.

eliminations.label: Point C (overshoot/peak) - Na+ maximal, K+ just starting
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the peak (~+35 mV) is maximal depolarization. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Na+ permeability is still dominant and K+ channels are only beginning to open. ✅ Correct if: asked for the point of maximal Na+ permeability / the overshoot.

eliminations.label: Point E (hyperpolarization) - K+ channels closing
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: after repolarization, slow voltage-gated K+ channels stay open briefly, dipping the membrane toward the K+ equilibrium (~-85 mV). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: K+ permeance is already DECLINING from its repolarization peak here. ✅ Correct if: asked why the membrane briefly hyperpolarizes below the resting potential.

reasoning: Label the phases: A resting, B depolarization, C overshoot, D repolarization, E hyperpolarization
Repolarization (D) opens voltage-gated K+ channels widely -> K+ efflux and K+ permeance peak, exceeding the resting level
-> the membrane is most permeable to K+ at D
mechanism: Resting potential (~-70 mV) is set by K+ leak channels, so at rest the membrane is more permeable to K+ than to Na+. The upstroke (depolarization, B) is produced by voltage-gated Na+ channels, making Na+ permeability dominant through the overshoot/peak (~+35 mV, C). Repolarization (D) follows as Na+ channels inactivate and voltage-gated K+ channels open fully; the resulting K+ efflux drives the membrane potential back down, and K+ permeance at this point EXCEEDS the resting value - the absolute maximum. Hyperpolarization (E) occurs because these K+ channels close slowly, transiently approaching the K+ equilibrium potential (~-85 mV) before the resting state is restored.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Physio > action potential phases and ion permeabilities: resting K+ leak; depolarization = voltage-gated Na+; repolarization = voltage-gated K+ (K+ permeance peaks, exceeds rest); hyperpolarization approaches E_K (-85 mV).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Recognize the question asks for ABSOLUTE K+ permeability across the whole cycle, not 'where does K+ dominate Na+.' That points to repolarization (D), not resting (A).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on depolarization, repolarization, overshoot, hyperpolarization, equilibrium potential, or voltage-gated vs leak K+ channels? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Resting point A is the classic trap - K+ dominates Na+ there, but the absolute K+ permeance peak rides the repolarization downstroke (D).

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The graph is a standard action-potential tracing; match each labeled point to its phase (A rest, B up, C peak, D down, E undershoot). If you misread which point is the downstroke, the physiology won't save you - re-anchor A on the baseline and D on the falling limb.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: K+ permeability PEAKS at repolarization (D); it only DOMINATES (vs Na+) at rest.

keywords.front: Membrane MOST permeable to K+ (absolute peak)

keywords.back: Slow K+ channel closure; approaches E_K (-85 mV)

firstAid.detail: Physio (Neuro) > repolarization = voltage-gated K+ channels open; K+ permeance peaks above resting; K+ efflux returns Vm toward rest.
firstAid.location: Physio (Neuro) > repolarization = voltage-gated K+ channels open; K+ permeance peaks above resting; K+ efflux returns Vm toward rest.

firstAid.detail: Physio > resting (K+ leak), depolarization (Na+ in), overshoot (peak Na+), repolarization (K+ out), hyperpolarization (toward E_K).
firstAid.location: Physio > resting (K+ leak), depolarization (Na+ in), overshoot (peak Na+), repolarization (K+ out), hyperpolarization (toward E_K).

firstAid.detail: Physio > E_K ~ -90 mV, E_Na ~ +60 mV; Nernst/Goldman; leak vs voltage-gated channels.
firstAid.location: Physio > E_K ~ -90 mV, E_Na ~ +60 mV; Nernst/Goldman; leak vs voltage-gated channels.

memoryHook: K+ goes OUT to bring you DOWN: the K+ permeability peak rides the repolarization downstroke (D), not the resting flat line (A).
